documentTitle: AOT vs JIT: When Does Code Get Translated to Machine Language?
h1Highlight: before it runs, or while it runs?
lede: Every program eventually has to be translated from source code into instructions the CPU understands. The question is: when does that translation happen? An Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compiler finishes the whole job once, before the program ever runs at all. A Just-in-Time (JIT) compiler puts it off until the program is actually running, then translates the most-used parts gradually, while watching the program's real behavior. Try the four scenarios below — the AOT pipeline, the JIT pipeline, the impact on startup time, and why JIT can sometimes produce code that's even faster than AOT.

aotPipeline.title: A developer writes source code
aotPipeline.desc: A program is written in a language like C, C++, Go, or Rust. This source code can't be run by the CPU at all yet — a CPU only understands machine-code instructions, not source code text.
fields: not yet executable by the CPU

aotPipeline.title: The AOT compiler translates the ENTIRE program at once, before it ever runs
aotPipeline.desc: At build/compile time — long before the program is ever launched — the AOT compiler reads the whole source code and fully translates it into machine-code instructions, targeted at a specific CPU architecture (say, x86-64 or ARM64).
fields: compile time — before the program runs

aotPipeline.title: Optimizations are based only on what can be read from the code itself
aotPipeline.desc: The compiler applies optimizations like removing code that's never used (dead code elimination) or computing constant values upfront (constant folding) — but all of it is based on static analysis of the code alone, since the compiler has never actually seen this program run.
fields: static — based on code analysis, not real behavior
has no idea how the program will actually be used

aotPipeline.title: The result: one native binary file, ready to go
aotPipeline.desc: The output of this process is a standalone executable file that's already fully machine-code instructions. This compilation step happens only ONCE, at build time — it will never repeat every time the program is launched.

aotPipeline.title: When launched, the CPU executes native code directly — no extra compilation
aotPipeline.desc: When the user opens the program, the operating system loads this native binary straight into memory, and the CPU immediately starts executing machine-code instructions. Not a single translation step happens at this point.
fields: full speed from the very first instruction

aotPipeline.title: All the heavy thinking happened upfront, just once
aotPipeline.desc: With AOT, the entire cost of compiling and optimizing is paid upfront, before the program is ever used by anyone — the trade-off is that the compiler can only optimize based on what it can guess from the code, not from how the program is actually used.
fields: the program's actual real-world usage pattern

jitPipeline.title: Source code is compiled to bytecode, not straight to machine code
jitPipeline.desc: A language like Java or C# is compiled at build time, but not into native machine code — instead, into bytecode (e.g. Java bytecode or .NET IL): a portable intermediate form that isn't tied to any specific CPU yet.
fields: bytecode / IR, not native machine code

jitPipeline.title: When the program starts, the runtime executes bytecode through an interpreter
jitPipeline.desc: The virtual machine (say, the JVM or the CLR) starts reading the bytecode one instruction at a time through an interpreter, translating it on the fly every time it runs. Flexible, but relatively slow compared to native code.
fields: interpreter — read & run one instruction at a time

jitPipeline.title: The runtime quietly counts which parts are called the most
jitPipeline.desc: While interpreting, the runtime is also gathering statistics: which functions or loops keep getting called over and over ('hot') versus code that barely ever runs ('cold').
fields: find the code that's most worth optimizing

jitPipeline.title: A function has now been called thousands of times — the JIT compiler steps in
jitPipeline.desc: Once a function crosses a call-count threshold (say, over 10,000 calls), the JIT compiler compiles JUST that function into native machine code — done while the program is still running, in the background.
fields: at runtime, while the program keeps running

jitPipeline.title: From now on, calling that function jumps straight to the native version
jitPipeline.desc: Every time that 'hot' function gets called again, the program now uses the freshly compiled native machine code — no longer going through the interpreter. For this function, its speed now matches AOT-compiled code.
fields: native code produced by the JIT compiler
still run through the interpreter as usual

jitPipeline.title: Rarely-used code may stay interpreted forever
jitPipeline.desc: Parts of the program that barely ever run might never get compiled at all — because the cost of compiling them wouldn't be worth the benefit. JIT only spends compilation effort where it's actually proven to pay off.
fields: only compile where it's proven to be worth it

warmup.title: Two versions of the same program, launched at the same moment
warmup.desc: Version A is AOT-compiled, version B is JIT-compiled. Both are running the exact same task, starting at the exact same second — let's compare how fast each one gets 'up to full speed'.

warmup.title: The AOT version runs at full speed immediately, from the very first instruction
warmup.desc: Since there's nothing left to compile, the AOT version runs at full speed the moment its first line of code executes. There's no 'warm-up' period at all.
fields: it was already native code before it even launched

warmup.title: The JIT version actually starts out slower — it's interpreting
warmup.desc: At that same moment, the JIT version is still running everything through the interpreter, because the JIT compiler hasn't had a chance to compile anything yet. Its speed is well below the AOT version, for now.
fields: well below AOT — still being interpreted
no code has been flagged 'hot' enough to compile yet

warmup.title: The JIT compiler has now found & compiled the hot spots
warmup.desc: After a few seconds of running, the runtime has gathered enough profiling data and compiled its hot paths into native code. The JIT version's speed jumps significantly as execution shifts from the interpreter to native code.
fields: hot paths detected & compiled to native code

warmup.title: After warming up, JIT's speed catches up to — and can even beat — AOT
warmup.desc: After running long enough, with all the important parts now compiled, the JIT version runs just as fast as (or in some cases, faster than) the AOT version — but it took time to get there.
fields: on par with, or faster than, AOT

warmup.title: AOT wins early, JIT can win over the long run
warmup.desc: AOT suits short-lived programs that need to be fast right away — CLI tools, serverless functions, command-line apps — since there's no time to warm up. JIT suits long-running programs — servers, desktop apps — where the upfront warm-up cost gets fully paid off over a long run.
fields: short-lived programs that need speed from second one
long-running programs where warm-up cost pays off over time

adaptive.title: The function add(a, b) is called thousands of times — always with integers
adaptive.desc: While the program runs, the function add(a, b) has already been called thousands of times, and it just so happens EVERY call so far has passed two integers as arguments — never any other type.

adaptive.title: An AOT compiler has no way of knowing this pattern in advance
adaptive.desc: An AOT compiler only works from source code, long before the program ever runs — it has no way of knowing this function will always end up being called with integers. So it must generate code general enough to safely handle any valid type, just in case.
fields: only what's written in the code, not the real usage pattern
generates general-purpose code that handles every possible type

adaptive.title: The JIT compiler watches the program actually running, in real time
adaptive.desc: The JIT compiler has an advantage AOT doesn't: it can see what's really happening. It records that add(a, b) has been called 50,000 times, and not once with anything other than an integer.
fields: sees real behavior instead of just guessing from the code

adaptive.title: JIT produces a highly specialized, fast version, speculating the type will always be integer
adaptive.desc: Armed with that observation, the JIT compiler generates a highly optimized native version — speculating that the arguments will always be integers, and skipping all the generic type-checking overhead AOT is forced to include for safety.
fields: very fast, specialized for the integer case
only correct for as long as that assumption holds

adaptive.title: At some point, add() gets called with a decimal number — the assumption breaks
adaptive.desc: Later on, add() ends up getting called once with a floating-point number instead of an integer. The specialized code can't safely handle that — the runtime detects the mismatch, throws away (deoptimizes) that specialized code, and temporarily falls back to the safe, general version for this call.
fields: the argument type broke the assumption (float, not integer)
deoptimization — discard the specialized code, fall back to the safe version

adaptive.title: JIT's superpower and its price, in one package: faster code, but it needs a safety net
adaptive.desc: This is exactly why JIT can sometimes produce faster code than AOT: it can safely speculate based on real observed behavior, something AOT simply cannot do. The price: JIT needs extra machinery (continuous profiling + deoptimization) that an AOT-compiled program never needs at all.
fields: can beat AOT's speed via speculation based on real behavior
